 
Page1 
To: Octavian Carare, Supervisory Economist, Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, at 
Octavian.Carare@cfpb.gov or (202) 435–7700 
 
From: Mitchell Berger, (comments made in personal capacity), mazruia@hotmail.com 
 
Re: Request for Information (RFI) Regarding Medical Payment Products, July 12, 2023, 
https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2023/07/12/2023-14726/request-for-information-regarding-
medical-payment-products 
 
Dear Mr. Carare: In response to the above RFI, I write to encourage CFPB, Treasury and HHS to further 
study the implications of use of such medical payment products as specialty credit cards and installment 
financing among patients receiving treatment for mental health conditions and substance use disorders. 
Patients being treatment for behavioral health conditions may be more likely than others to have medical 
debt and may have trouble finding treatment fully covered by insurance, leading to use of other available 
payment approaches such as credit cards.1 
 
According to the 2021 National Survey on Drug Use and Health (the most recent survey available),2 46.3 
million Americans aged 12 and older had a substance use disorder and 57.8 million adults age 18 and 
older had a mental illness. Of these adults, roughly 14.1 million American adults had a serious mental 
illness which is differentiated from any mental illness based on causing serious functional impairment. 
Roughly 25 percent of those aged 12 and older with an untreated substance use disorder cite the cost of 
care as a key barrier to accessing treatment. Similarly, roughly 48 percent of adults with any mental 
illness and 54 percent of those with a serious mental illness cited cost of care as the key obstacle 
preventing their access to treatment.  
 
One factor contributing to these cost of care barriers is that many behavioral health professionals do not 
accept insurance, which in turn drives higher out-of-pocket costs for patients.3 Installment plans, medical 
credit cards and other approaches may be needed to defray these costs. Some behavioral health companies 
online expressly recommend use of products mentioned in the CFBP/HHS/Treasury RFI for addiction 
treatment or other behavioral health services. 
 
One 2020 Psychiatric Services study found that fewer than 6 percent of active practicing psychiatrists in 
Massachusetts filed insurance claims for more than 300 patients per year. The authors comment that 
“[e]ven in areas with relatively large numbers of psychiatrists, such as Massachusetts, most psychiatrists 
accept private health insurance for only a few patients per year, and few psychiatrists meet even 
conservative definitions of a full outpatient caseload of patients billed through private health insurance. 
We found that many psychiatrists who participate in the insurance market see very few patients with 
insurance reimbursement, which suggests that counting psychiatrists alone could grossly overestimate the 
supply available to patients who cannot afford to pay out of pocket.”4 Psychiatrists may receive lower 
reimbursement than other physicians even when providing similar treatments.5 The situation for other 
behavioral health providers is similar. Various studies note that: only about one-third of psychologists 
 
1 Grafova IB, Clifford PR, Hudson SV, Steinberg MB, O'Malley DM, Elliott J, Llanos AAM, Saraiya B, Duberstein PR. Disease and debt: 
Findings from the 2019 Panel Study of Income Dynamics in the United States. Prev Med. 2022 Nov;164:107248. doi: 
10.1016/j.ypmed.2022.107248; https://www.kff.org/health-costs/report/the-burden-of-medical-debt-results-from-the-kaiser-family-
foundationnew-york-times-medical-bills-survey/view/print/; https://www.npr.org/sections/health-shots/2022/10/19/1125446666/debt-mental-
health-care-u-s-families; Novak PJ, Ali MM, Sanmartin MX. Disparities in Medical Debt Among U.S. Adults with Serious Psychological 
Distress. Health Equity. 2020 Dec 30;4(1):549-555. doi: 10.1089/heq.2020.0090 
2 https://www.samhsa.gov/data/release/2021-national-survey-drug-use-and-health-nsduh-releases 
3 https://www.americanprogress.org/article/the-behavioral-health-care-affordability-problem/ 
4 Benson NM, Myong C, Newhouse JP, Fung V, Hsu J. Psychiatrist Participation in Private Health Insurance Markets: Paucity in the Land of 
Plenty. Psychiatr Serv. 2020 Dec 1;71(12):1232-1238. 
5 Mark TL, Olesiuk W, Ali MM, Sherman LJ, Mutter R, Teich JL. Differential Reimbursement of Psychiatric Services by Psychiatrists and Other 
Medical Providers. Psychiatr Serv. 2018 Mar 1;69(3):281-285. 

 
Page2 
accepting private insurance;6 government and private insurance often place limitations on addiction 
treatment;7 and in some states more than 40 percent of marriage and family therapists do not accept 
insurance.8 
 
These factors may contribute to use of medical payment products for mental health or substance use 
disorder treatment particularly for residential treatment or for what some view as innovative treatments 
that have yet to be FDA-approved.9 There also have been allegations that some substance use centers have 
encouraged use of loans or other financing not necessarily in the patient’s long-term best interests.10  
 
Overall, I think better understanding is needed of how these products are used in behavioral health 
settings and potential benefits and drawbacks for patients, families/caregivers and behavioral health 
providers. It would be helpful as well to better understand use of such products for residential behavioral 
health treatment; disparities in use of medical payment products for behavioral health care based on 
geography (rural, suburban, urban), race/ethnicity and income levels; and long-term impacts of use of 
credit cards, installment plans and other payment products for patients. Also important is better 
understanding the use of potential alternatives to pay for some forms of behavioral health care such as 
health savings accounts, which tend to be underused by many consumers.11 Raising awareness among 
providers of tools to promote patient financial security, such as lower income patient eligibility for federal 
and state tax credits,  can help perhaps in some cases to reduce patient need for less flexible financial 
tools to pay for medical expenses for behavioral health or other services.12 I appreciate your consideration 
of these views. 
 
Sincerely,  
Mitchell Berger 
 
Note/disclaimer: The above comments represent my personal views and should not be imputed to any 
other person nor to any public or private entity.

31;3:100051

Accessed Oct. 5, 202. Available at: https://crsreports.congress.gov; Crandall-Hollick M. The Child Tax Credit: How it works and who receives it, 
CRS Report, R41873, Jan. 12. https://crsreports.congress.gov; Topic No. 602 Child and Dependent Care Credit, Internal Revenue Service. Last

5, 2022. Available at: https://www.urban.org/policy-centers/cross-center-initiatives/state-and-local-finance-initiative/state-and-local-
backgrounders/state-earned-income-tax-credits; Berkowitz SA, Orr CJ, Palakshappa D. The Public Health Case for a Universalist Child Tax 
Credit. JAMA Pediatr. 2022;176(9):843-844. doi:10.1001/jamapediatrics.2022.2503  
 
 
Digitally signed by Mitchell Berger
